persona.name: Ayaka Nishimura

occupation.title: Freelance UX Designer
occupation.organization: Self-employed, contracts with 3-4 clients simultaneously through a Fukuoka-based design collective
occupation.description: Designs user interfaces and conducts usability research for mid-size e-commerce and SaaS companies. Works fully remote from her apartment in Fukuoka. Juggles multiple client projects, each with their own meeting cadence — some daily standups, some weekly syncs. Spends about 40% of her time in video calls and the rest in focused design work.

persona.education: B.A. in Visual Communication Design from Kyushu University. Self-taught in UX research methods through online courses and the Interaction Design Foundation. Attended a 3-month design bootcamp in San Francisco in 2020.
long_term_goals: Build a sustainable freelance practice earning 8 million yen annually
Become a recognized voice in the Japanese UX community through writing and speaking
Spend 2 months per year working from different Asian cities
Eventually launch a small UX consultancy with 2-3 partners
persona.style: Casual and warm, with a Hakata dialect that slips through when she's relaxed. Wears oversized linen shirts and round glasses. Her home office has an aesthetic that her Instagram followers admire: wooden desk, monstera plant, single-origin coffee setup. In meetings she's engaged and visual — shares her screen constantly to sketch ideas live. Writes client messages in polite but friendly Japanese, avoiding stiff keigo. Has a habit of nodding and saying 'naruhodo' while processing, which clients find reassuring.

traits: Adaptable to different client cultures and communication styles
Values autonomy and work-life integration over corporate stability
Enthusiastic early adopter of design and productivity tools
Occasionally disorganized with admin tasks like invoicing
Empathetic listener who picks up on unspoken client needs
Independent but sometimes lonely working from home

interests: Coffee brewing methods — currently into AeroPress
Film photography with a Contax T2
Fukuoka's food scene, especially yatai street stalls
Remote work lifestyle and digital nomad communities
Writing about UX process on note (Japanese Medium equivalent)
likes: Tools that save her from manual note-taking during client calls
Clients who trust her process and give design freedom
Late-night yatai ramen after a productive week
Finding unexpected connections between design research and other fields
dislikes: Tools that require enterprise contracts for a solo freelancer
Meetings that lack visual aids or shared documents
Being asked to take meeting notes on top of participating in the discussion
The loneliness of solo remote work on rainy days

beliefs: Good design starts with listening, and meeting notes are how you prove you listened
Freelancers need better tools than salaried workers because nobody else will organize things for them
Working from Fukuoka instead of Tokyo is a deliberate quality-of-life decision, not a compromise
AI should handle the tedious parts of work so humans can focus on the creative parts
skills: UI/UX design and prototyping in Figma
Visual facilitation and live sketching in workshops
Client relationship management as a solo practitioner

general: Records all client calls with permission and reviews key moments later
Uses Notion as her personal CRM, project tracker, and knowledge base
Shares meeting summaries with clients within 24 hours — considers it part of professionalism
Tests new AI tools on personal projects before introducing them to client workflows

morning: 30 minutes of journaling or reading design articles
Checks client messages on Slack and email
workday: 2-3 client calls scattered through the day
Design work in focused 90-minute blocks between calls
Lunch break: walks to a nearby cafe or cooks at home
evening: Yoga or a walk along the Naka River
Cooks dinner — simple Japanese home cooking
Browses design inspiration on Dribbble or reads
Occasionally writes a note.com article about UX process
weekend: Saturday: film photography walk around Fukuoka neighborhoods
Coffee with designer friends at a specialty shop
Sunday: admin catch-up (invoicing, expenses) — dreads it
Evening: visits a yatai for ramen and people-watching

health.physical: Generally healthy. Practices yoga 2-3 times a week. Gets stiff from desk work and invested in an ergonomic chair last year. Slight astigmatism — wears glasses for screens and driving.
health.mental: Mostly content with her lifestyle but experiences periodic isolation anxiety as a solo remote worker. The switch from agency to freelance two years ago was liberating but also removed her daily social structure. Manages through scheduled social interactions and the design collective's monthly meetups.

relationships.name: Kohei Shimizu
relationships.description: 34, web developer who also freelances. They live together and sometimes share clients. He handles the technical builds for her UX designs. They respect each other's work time and have a 'no Slack after 20:00' household rule.

relationships.name: Mai Yoshida
relationships.relation: Design collective co-member and close friend
relationships.description: 30, graphic designer. They co-founded the Fukuoka design collective and share a co-working desk twice a week for social interaction.

relationships.name: Setsuko Nishimura
relationships.description: 60, lives in Kumamoto. A retired school librarian who doesn't fully understand what UX design is but supports Ayaka unconditionally. They meet once a month.
